segments.text: remember that's the moment you've got about eight hours maximum from that moment from the moment of occlusion until you get irreversible necrosis welcome to the aesthetics mastery show I'm doctor Tim Pierce hi I'm around appears and today we're looking at when good clinicians get catastrophic ly bad outcomes from complications for some very interesting reasons I don't think people think about a lot I recently interviewed a responsible good clinician who took all the action that she would have thought was necessary at the time with reasonable training and got a very bad complication at the end and I was thinking as we discussed this as part of my complications mastery program we went through every detail of that case and there's so much learning but a lot of it is around the concepts other than how do you get the diagnosis right and how do you do the treatment there is a lot of other stuff that can completely change the outcome and that's why I want to

talk about today so it's a bit like from my perspective when you something goes wrong in your life and you think to yourself the reason that went wrong is because a series of hideous stars aligned and just everything went wrong at once but how does that even begin how does that happen yeah absolutely I think it's that analogy both of us once were so independently saw two different health and safety lectures didn't we and they use in the analogy of the Swiss cheese which is all the holes need to align for something really bad to happen I actually quite like the analogy of a lock and if you ever look inside a lock which hopefully will get a picture up to show people what actually happens when you unlock something with a key is that the key pushes up these little pins all of them have to be at exactly the right level and then you can unlock the lock unfortunately what we're doing is unlocking a little mini catastrophe when it comes to these really serious complications but what I've noticed by

dissecting these cases which as you know I do that for the computations mastery program where we interview if either a patient or a clinician and go in depth and into the case to figure out all that if bits of learning we can get and that could have gone wrong so we've been doing that for a while and recently I interviewed someone who had a really bad complication with the nose this is a good studious clinician who was following guidelines and still got a really bad outcome and we discussed this openly I mean obviously she's not going to put on social media but I'm so grateful for people who do share that kind of thing and I'm going to share some of the broader principles today so that you can all think a bit more clearly around the types of things that put a good clinician of course and when they're doing their best and really bad outcomes cuz it'll happen and so that's the key it's almost like you're walking in the woods or something and the cut you come to a fork in the road and you've got it this is what happens in real life case studies that went bad things go wrong clinically and you have a choice to do you think

you shared actually with your first vo where there was a moment actually it was before the Easter weekend and you kind of thought oh it'll be fine and then you thought no go back and check and that was a fork in the road where you had the chance to go wrong and you didn't so absolutely so in between you seeing a patient getting the diagnosis correct and rescuing them there are probably about a hundred and fifty folks in the road and each one of them is a little mini challenge about to block you from getting the right answer and just by understanding the types of problems that present themselves and how it can sway you in a different direction you'll be more on your guard and as you rightly pointed out the first one is your own inner desire not to have to deal with the bus refusing because they never have but a bit like children they never arrive you know when your life is peaceful and calm and you've got plenty of time to deal with it never feels like the right time so that that's the first that one of the many aspects that not people of course but there are actually many much more almost more mundane things one of the

examples I've come across recently would be running out of a particular size of cannula so just switching to a different sized cannula and for some people it makes well it makes it more likely if that candle is a bit smaller as one of the parts of this case was maybe using a different size cannula now of course you never know for sure whether that was a deciding factor but it was certainly one thing in favor of not having the complication in the first place so and this is a lot away but why would that be a problem well the smaller camera it is the sharper is so the closer it gets to being effectively like a needle but the downside with Canada's are much longer they tend to stay in the same place so much more which means the size of the occlusion is much more so unfortunately cannulas often not perceived as much safer and they probably are less likely to cause a very occlusion but on only thinking from first principles here but if you do have a vested collusion they're probably a bit more likely to cause a really big faster occasion and that's what happened in this particular case it was a nose case was yeah so but lots more other interesting stuff that went around that so I wanted to talk around

particularly the emotions there's you've already brought up my own emotion at that time when I first had a vascular occlusion was this little question of it'll be fine like I wanted to reassure myself so that would be the first thing I'd say is look out for your own desire to reassure yourself because we all have it none of us want this to happen to ourselves so your brain you know the reticular activating system is always looking for things that are relevant to what it wants that's how our brains work now we also have our fears and it also will look for things that it wants to avoid so you need to just keep that alert in your mind that if you get it wrong at this point and it's another five hours before you see your patient back again you are already massively on the backfoot so it'll just knowing that if you're just by watching this video you remember that you're much more likely to be looking for the right things in that situation knowing the self-deception is something we all do when we would like to avoid a complication so when you want to reassure yourself you look for things that are positive and we this is such a human thing to do it'll be fine it's

very unlikely I only injected a small amount oh she's a worrier yeah so this is the patient psychology which is which was also another part of this case it's not been you can think of patients as kind of to worrying too much worrying too little or maybe somewhere in between and I've already said too I mean I remember talking to one of my patients is very laid-back trust me immensely so she's always like I don't I know you're safe and you're a good practitioner and I'm not worried and I'm not a warrior and I'm immediately thinking the day she messages me with even one little question I'm getting back into the clinic to review because I know that's all true she's not warrior which means if she gives me a small signal I need to make sure that I react really strongly to that whereas I do have other patients who worry about everything and there's a million questions and I'm more likely to take that into account but there's also pitfall there which is you can't disregard them just because they've always been asking loads and loads of questions because you never know you need to ask them the precise set of questions that you would for anyone and it all comes back to their signal-to-noise ratio which is an ideal

I want to kind of bring up in this podcast quite a lot which is the idea that there's there are certain things that outweigh everything else that you need to search for and not get blocked by the many challenges which of these little Forks in the road so what is this the truth the truest signal you can get and how can you be using that to define your actions apart from all the other human stuff that gets in the way the noise yeah so some of the other noise which was also true in this case was just the practicalities of getting the patient back in front of you and this is something in links to the inconvenience as well which is you have to be ready to be very inconvenienced if you want to be a safe practitioner I remember saying it to myself I'm going to be inconvenienced if someone messages me this before the days of whatsapp so we didn't have the luxury of getting in a video of Hilary fell so I was like okay I'm going to be inconvenience three or four times a year I'm going to go out and check on a nervous patient who's got a bruise and I know it's going to be a waste of my time in that instance but the practice over

the year I might pick up a vo and not have a problem I think that kind of thing it sounds small but it's not it's so big telling yourself ah yes in the future this will happen I always think that when you tell the story about how your vo took an hour and a half to return to reverse I that went into my brain is like oh right okay doctor Tim he took an hour and a half so that's all that's what I can expect and it makes it more normalized it's good to say it up front well the one before that took 45 minutes so I was actually starting to get pretty stressed out by the time about two and a half now I've also know of cases now they've gone on for three or four hours so don't let the time hold you back just be aware that it takes time but it's exactly that kind of thing that makes a big difference so what we've touched on so I was just gonna think of some more mundane things that might make a difference it could be that your room layout changes so I certainly felt this working in different venues that's that suddenly you're doing your injections in a different way and this can make you do something that

can this can make you do something that you wouldn't normally do so little things that are probably okay you know particularly you've got loads of experience you probably know enough but there was you know there have been times when I've kind of leaned over and a more awkward position maybe your angle of entries not quite as simple as it would have been with the way you've done 99% of your injections these are little things that will let you into different areas of risk and you don't always appreciate it so they get a complication and most times you get away with it but it's often a factor that you've done something different that's unusual to you different products so this was also another interesting part of this case I reviewed which is using a different product and certainly just creates an element of doubt you know you might do things differently there's a question mark about how much dissolving agent you need maybe if you've always used one product and you know roughly what it takes to dissolve that and you're suddenly using something totally different it just creates more uncertainty you don't know you don't have the sense of confidence in what you're doing in the same way because it's uncharted territory effectively let's say the patient is lay back and

they've also put time pressure on you know like I've got you know I've got to be or you know I can't be bruised or I'll come on to be fine you know it's a huge thing often it's you we all want our patients to be happy and they if they start to get an angsty like they just want to move on and that's more noise that you've got to separate from the signal because remembering that getting it wrong at this stage causes weeks months and even years of real unhappiness you've got to block out those little drivers to solve the immediate problem and keep your eye on the main goal and it's one of the things I noticed actually which is my partner one of them one of the videos we had that I helped no.10 ik had a partner who didn't know she was having treatment so we were confronting the fact that she was getting more and more anxious that he wouldn't need to know where she was and then she'd have to come clean and not just that but there was also a complication and then he'd be like I told you not to do this you know all those kind of dynamics so you you've got to drown out that noise because that

noise if you get swept up in it and she's getting nervous and she's on her phone or he's on her phone messaging and texting and you're like you really want to avoid that for her because that suddenly become her main concern you've got to say look you can solve that later even if you never have treatment again let's get you through this with no risk of a scar yes that's a hundred times worse than the relationship issues that you know there relate the scar will last quite possibly might last longer than the relationship so you've got to think very long-term and just remember it as noise like okay fair enough you've got relationship problems my main priority is making sure you don't scar I've been disclosed exactly I think and I think that you as clinicians you know from my late perspective I a clinician was firm with me ultimately I would respect that I'm not gonna start arguing if you say this is the thing we're dealing with now this is the most important thing I need you to work with me on this I think that they will respect that yeah absolutely and but repeating it's important and just being aware of it because you don't want

was basically a fairly poor technique in the way that most people would it's a little touch like that not of yours there the nose lady yes so she's asking for help it's what we all do these days but you need to get your patient to do it really especially if you're not gonna see them in person you need to be absolutely sure not kind of looks okay to me and the patient's not doing it properly I'm pretty sure this is gonna happen all over the place because what's up you feel like you can make the process easier but the patient's the one doing the co2 refill and unless you are really certain that they are doing a phenomenally good job in great lighting and you just be you need to be very cautious about reassuring them not to come in because remember that's the moment you've got about eight hours maximum probably a bit less than that from that moment from the moment of occlusion until you get irreversible necrosis so it you yeah once you build that into the equation that's one of the most important aspects is the time doesn't give you room for mistakes and it depends what your

background is I know as a GP we use time all the time as a diagnostic tool basically let's see what happens you can't do that inclusion has different philosophy all this isn't it ya know the philosophy is it's well anything that's not 100% reassure well you should get them back inside back in front of you but also I'd say a lot of clinicians don't do great we refill because it's different in different places like on lips it's relatively easy I'd say one of the biggest mistakes is simply not compressing enough blood out of the skin before you release so that's a time and pressure effect and also the area so I often find it's easier had a suspected vascular occlusion in the cheek and cheeks are quite kind of full and fat filled and kind of touching around here just wasn't that reassuring compressing the whole of the facial artery and really pushing on it for like 5 seconds and then releasing it you could literally see the blood flow run rush back in and a very clear way extremely reassuring and I've also picked up vascular occlusion in the chin that was

you just aren't as sensitive to the blood flow question what about and diagnostic questions like pain and then maybe they don't have the pain which you know to be one of the other but then you're they didn't uh pain did she yeah so that's another great and well she didn't have it immediately I do think Isis I suspect that if you could get the data that nearly all patients with a true vascular fusion will experience pain at the point of necrosis occurring because it's dying tissue I mean it's kind of weird if you don't experience anything and it's quite built into our evolution that when tissue is dying you feel some sort of pain but I bet it's not a hundred percent so but it will be up there I would rate pain at some point on the cycle as a really significant factor but the interesting thing is about this particular case as well is that the pain Pete and then started to improve according to the patient now I suspect a laid-back patient who's trying to reassure herself will pull out those things and tell you the reassuring stuff and I think that's one of the things
